dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 responding for Convulsions/Seizure.
Hi, I'm Taylor, an EMT with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what happened?
He began shaking uncontrollably and vomiting about 15 minutes ago on the patio of the National Theater. I caught him as he started to collapse.
Mr. Smith, can you open your eyes for me?
Yeah, okay.
What city are we in?
Richmond.
Mr. Smith, can you squeeze my finger?
Okay.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me what you were doing before the seizure?
He was with me at the theater. He started going to his knees, and I pulled him down before he hit the ground. Then he had a seizure.
Your airway looks okay, but you did vomit earlier. I'm going to turn you slightly onto your side.
Your respiratory rate is 20, which is within the normal range.
I can check your blood pressure now, sir.
It's at 131 over 71, Mr. Smith. You okay with that?
We've cleared your airway-though you were close to having it blocked by vomit earlier.
Your oxygen saturation is at 94%.
Normal chest movement with equal rise on both sides. No signs of trouble.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me what day it is today?
I think it's Tuesday.
I'd like you to count from 1 to 10 and then repeat after me, "Do not let go of the phone."
Fine, I can do that.
Mr. Smith, I'm going to check your blood sugar now. It might feel like a slight pinch.
Blood glucose is at 102-normal levels.
Strong regular radial pulse. Your skin is pale, warm, and slightly clammy.
Your heart rate is very high at 131-faster than normal.
Your pupils are 7-mm, equal, and reactive to light.
We've maintained your airway and will continue monitoring for any changes.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me what you were doing right before the seizure?
I was at the theater. I had a drink and took a tab of something-probably acid-around 7:45.
Were you feeling okay before it happened?
I was feeling a bit off after the drink-sweating more than usual.
Did you have a seizure before?
Once. About 10 years ago I had a brain bleed and then a seizure. No meds ever since.
When was the last time you ate or drank?
A beer an hour back, and a tab of acid-probably around the same time.
I'll perform a 4-lead EKG to check your heart rhythm.
Performed 4-lead EKG. Rhythm shows sinus tachycardia-nothing too alarmingly wrong here.
I'll get a 12-lead EKG now for more details.
Got the 12-lead EKG. It shows sinus tach with a small T wave abnormality and a long QT interval.
We're going to do ETCO2 capnography now to assess gas exchange.
ETCO2 reading is 35-within a normal range, no acidosis or hypercarbia detected.
I'm going to try placing an IV on your left arm. This is to administer fluids if needed.
IV insertion was unsuccessful on the first attempt. I'll try another site if needed during transport.
Your skin remains pale, warm, and slightly clammy. Capillary refill is less than two seconds.
Your level of alertness and recognition has improved since we started. You are oriented to person, place, and today's date now.
We're going to roll you onto the stretcher now. Please stay still. This will keep transport safe.
You're now secured on the stretcher. Please remain still during the ride to prevent movement.
I will. Thanks for being careful with me.
Glucose has dropped to zero now during transport-possibly due to repeated testing or monitoring issues.
EKG still reflects sinus tachycardia-no acute change in rhythm observed so far.
Heart rate decreased to 115-improvement from earlier readings.
Breathing is steady at 18 per minute-consistent with room air breathing and no signs of labored work.
Blood pressure is 118 over 65-lower than initial, but still within acceptable range for hypoperfusion assessment.
Oxygen saturation is at 95% now-staying stable and no indication of hypoxia at this time.
Your alertness is better now. From GCS 13 to 15-great improvement according to the evolution we've tracked so far.
Mr. Smith, where would you prefer to go for treatment today?
VCU is fine. I trust the people there.
Perfect, we're headed to VCU Medical Center. Let me know if you need anything during the ride.
You're now on the Reeves and secure for transport-nothing holding you in place can interfere with your stability during travel.
Let me know if you feel any pressure or pain during this maneuver. We can adjust the harness if needed.
No issues-just keep going. I'm comfortable with where I am right now.
Nurse, this is a 24-year-old male who had a seizure on the patio and was vomiting-alertness returned within minutes.
GCS improved from 13 to 15; no motor impairments noted. Substance use-alcohol and a tab of acid-reported by the patient himself.
No pain reported-pain level zero. EKG shows sinus tach with T wave changes and long QT interval-will provide you with the tracing in a second.
IV start was unsuccessful-we attempted once but had no lock today. Vital signs all remain stable per our short field transport.

Past medical history includes a brain bleed and a seizure 10 years ago-no medications taken. Per protocol, we used the altered mental status (Protocol 3-15) field management procedures to clear the airway and monitor for further complications.
Thanks for the detailed report. We'll handle it from here. Keep that stretcher secured, and drive smart through these roads tonight.
You all did great. Thanks for helping me through this-felt really shaken up, but I'm okay now.
